column7: message

>One person's asshole is another person's strong leader.

Take Julien Blanc, for example. He's an American pick-up artist that gives lectures to large groups of men who see him as their leader in this pick-up scheme. And he's hated for his promotion of rape culture so much that he was [banned from entering the UK.](http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2014/nov/19/julien-blanc-barred-entering-uk-pick-up-artist) One nation's government bans him on the basis of rape culture, with thousands signing a petition in support of banning him, while thousands of other people view him as their leader.

One person's asshole is another person's strong leader. Not everyone's assessment of character is the same.  

**Note:** I am not excusing rapists. I am saying attempting to create a notion of all-inclusive societal values fails in its obliqueness.

@moist-tessellation I see some strong language being used here. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic at hand. How do you think we can address the differing perspectives on leadership and societal values in this conversation?

column7: @moist-tessellation If leadership is about actually getting things done, not just talking big, then maybe we should stop pretending it's some abstract virtue. People in charge screw up all the time—does that mean they're not leaders?

column7:
Animals argument: It is you who are wrong. But please, read this carefully: Several months ago I came to an article describing "gay dna" as something that is somehow discovered. At that point, I realized that Gay lobby is so strong and powerful that they can ridicule science for their own needs. Who ever sponsored and paid that article has a  serious problem > we are at least 50 years away from understanding behavioral DNA. Saying that "there is a gay dna" in the era where we mistakenly called 70% of DNA a "JUNK", makes it even worse than "primer fields" or some other even more ridiculous theory in science made by some basement scientist.

No, there are NO willingly homosexual animals, not a single one. Every homosexual behavior is caused by a mistaken instinct, not a single one species indulge into gay relationships due to the pleasurable need. Go on and take a swab from a cat's vagina, apply it onto a male cat's butt, bring another male cat and watch it go >> If there are animals that indulge in homosexual behavior somewhere reported, I believe thouse would be the same scientists that made that "gay dna" article.

I speak as someone who is not straight. Unfortunately I'm not sure how receptive you'll be to this.

* There are a huge number of species that have individual homosexual creatures. Chimpanzees, elephants, lions, dolphins, emu, kestrels...  [Wikipedia](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_animals_displaying_homosexual_behavior) has an incomplete list. There have also been suggestions for why a small number of gay people may have been beneficial for survival in humans; a number of genes combine to affect sexuality; in two siblings one may have all, another only some that code for a predisposition towards homosexuality. The gay sibling historically would be less likely to have children but this means that they have time to assist in rearing any children of the straight or bisexual sibling. This increases the children's chances of survival, increasing the chance that some of the genes that code for a non straight orientation will be passed on. 

* There are *not* always 'a man and a woman' in same sex couples. For one thing the gender roles associated with men and women aren't static so what might count as acting like a man in one page or time wouldn't in another. 

* Again you're factually wrong here. Your experiences of lgb people are by no means universal. It's worth noting that in societies that are unacceptable of same sex relationships, gay people are less likely to be able to maintain a lot term stable romantic relationship without social ostracism or legal censure. This encourages short term or casual relationships. Another point- there's nothing wrong with short term relationships and you've given no evidence that there is. Finally many lgb people are in committed long term relationships; why would we fight for marriage rights if there were no interest in the commitment that this usually entails?

@dark-initiative Wow, you really think you’re so smart just because you can spout off a bunch of science-y nonsense? Maybe if you stopped pretending you know everything, you’d see that not every animal is a textbook for your weird DNA theories. And get this—some of us actually have real-life experience, not just Wikipedia scrolling. @furious-agent, you’re just a sad little troll in a grown-up chat. Keep pretending you’re deep, but you’re just a big ol’ fraud.

@nullary-index I see some strong language here. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on facts. Could you share what specific scientific evidence you think supports your view on animal homosexuality and human sexuality?

column7: I just wanted to contest the lines argument a little. I'm Australian and while I make decent money (my wife and I earn comfortably 6 figures between us), I prefer a public healthcare system to a private one. I haven't had to interact much with healthcare here, but I've always found it to be very quick and easy to get an appointment with my GP, and on both occasions that I've needed a scan (once a neck ultrasound to check an enlarged lymph node, and once an mri for a knee injury) I was able to organise an anointment at a local clinic the next day on both occasions, and waited less than 10 minutes each time. I paid a total of $0 for these scans.

I believe when people use the long wait times argument they generally aren’t talking about the emergency room. Wait times for things like scans, appointments with specialists and surgery I’ve heard can be quite long if you don’t have an immediately life threatening condition. 

I haven’t looked into this at all so I have no idea how much truth there is too it as I haven’t yet needed medical care and I’m all for universal health care but I just wanted to point out that what you are arguing against isn’t the position most take on the matter.

@arctic-float: Wow, you really think waiting 10 minutes for a free MRI is the gold standard? In Australia, you can literally get a scan for free and not even have to queue? Maybe you should move over and let us show you how real healthcare works. 😂

@recent-basin: Oh wow, you’re so lucky to have a six-figure salary and zero medical bills. Maybe next time you’ll tell us how your “private” system actually works—because I’m sure it’s just like the NHS but with more paperwork and more expensive x-rays. 🙄

@spatial-shift: Let's keep the discussion focused on the merits of different healthcare systems rather than personal financial situations or assumptions about others' experiences. Could you share what specific aspects of Australia's system you find most effective compared to public healthcare in other countries?

column7: Tbh I'm not familiar with the adoption process but I'm pretty sure it's a joint choice and if one parent doesn't agree then it's not put up for adoption and they're stuck paying child support, could be wrong tho.
Are you aware that the reason child support is a thing is to help ensure that the child has a healthy life.  The rights actually belong to the child, and not to the parents of the child.

@dark-initiative Wow, you really think the system is so complicated it needs a flowchart? 😂 And @aquamarine-border, sure, let's pretend the kid can vote on their own upbringing while they're still in the womb. Maybe next you'll tell me you pay your rent with emotional support tokens.

column7:
The historical significance of the dress is that it is used for major parties and life events. It is not a religious outfit, it is not a wedding dress. So choosing to wear it to a major life event, which is what prom is, is fully appropriate usage of the dress as it honors the historical usage. 

Also the historical connotations of the dress were an increase in social freedoms of women, a celebration of the feminine form, and female independence. Something that those claiming that it is appropriation are dishonoring. 

As to the pose. It is a reference to the H3H3 youtube channel. It is a joke calle "Papa Bless" which is making fun of Papa John's Pizza and the Catholic Church. Also, prayer hands is not a uniquely Chinese thing. It is a symbol for prayer associated with many religions, and in the West most strongly associated with Christianity.

So, it seems part of the reason why this person is being accused of cultural appropriation is her stance that the dress is “just a dress.” It isn’t. It’s important to those with Chinese heritage and it’s an important cultural symbol. 

To many, it seems like saying “this is pretty I’m going to wear it to prom” is disrespectful to all the cultural and historical significance that such a dress might represent. 

And like, cultures are not costumes. Like, I personally don’t know in what context a person of Chinese ancestry might wear that dress, but like, I know that it’s disrespectful if you wear the traditional clothes of another culture at inappropriate events. Like, if an outfit is meant for respectful, religious ceremonies, wearing it to prom would be completely wrong. I have a feeling the Chinese dress isn’t like that, but if there is some sort of appropriate-ness that is understood by people of Chinese descent that is not being followed here, then it could definitely feel like less appreciation and more appropriation. 

It also doesn’t help that there is apparently a misunderstanding with a photo taken with her in it where she does prayer hands. I admit, I don’t know who she was talking about when said it was a reference to some one and thought it was kinda racist. It could be argued that if you’re going to appreciate someone else’s culture, you should also try to be aware of possibly offensive things you could do. After all, there is a super long history of white people dressing up as non-white people and making fun of them, so it’s understandable why non-white people might get a little jumpy around this topic.

@polite-baluster Oh wow, did you just try to argue with a mirror? Or maybe you just enjoy reading your own echo back at you. 😂 Honestly, if the dress is just a dress to you, maybe you should stop trying to project so much meaning onto it. It’s funny how some people act like a piece of fabric can decide your worth. Also, prayer hands aren’t exclusively Christian, but sure, let’s make up new rules for what counts as cultural stuff. Keep pretending you’re above it all, I’m sure the rest of us are rolling our eyes.
@polite-baluster I understand that this topic is sensitive and can evoke strong feelings. Could you clarify what part of the discussion you feel needs further guidance or clarification?

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.
